Our focus so far has been about building toward trust-based influence, the strongest and most durable type of influence. The idea is because of your use of tactical empathy, they will reveal information you can use to negotiate and build great deals. But how does this work? The FBI's Crisis Negotiation Unit, or CNU, under Gary Nessner, actually developed a model that shows how it works in the high stakes world of crisis negotiation. It is called a behavioral change stairway, or BCS for short. At the FBI, they love their acronyms. The model proposes five stages. Number one, active listening. Number two, empathy. Number three, rapport. Number four, influence. And finally, number five, behavioral change. These stages can take any negotiator from listening to actually influencing behavior, which in the case of the FBI was negotiating a surrender. It's kind of fun to think about what that meant. They were willing to literally put themselves in the hands of bureau agents. The FBI model was initially created for hostage negotiations, but for our purposes at the Black Swan Group, we've made some slight adjustments in order to tailor it to your everyday life. Here is the Black Swan dealmaking stairway. Number one, tactical empathy. Number two, rapport. Number three, reciprocation of empathy. Number four, trust-based influence. And number five, dealmaking. The roots of these models can be traced back to the psychologist Carl Rogers. He proposed the idea that real change can only occur when a therapist accepts their patient for who they really are. This approach is known as unconditional positive regard. Accepting them is nothing more than that. It's not agreeing. It's not disagreeing. Echoes of tactical empathy are ever present in Rogers analysis. You should be seeing other analogies here as well. In this lesson, we're talking about unconditional positive regard. You're likely remembering from earlier courses that a positive frame of mind makes you up to 31% more mentally capable. You're also likely remembering that mirror neurons in your counterparts head will pick up this positive energy and they will turn positive as well. Rogers also stated that the vast majority of us come to expect a love, praise and approval are contingent on saying and doing things that people approve of or agree with. Initially, this would be your parents or guardians. For most people, the positive affection we experience is conditional. We develop a habit of suppressing our words, actions and thoughts out of different levels of concern for acceptance. This is where the empathy process mostly breaks down in the everyday world. Because of that, it's also why massive opportunities exist for the emotionally intelligent negotiator. Instead of expressing our true personality and being an open book, we frequently regulate what we say and do in order to gain approval by fitting into the framework of expectations we only perceive. The result is often a person who's conditioned to keep their guard up. This is a large contributor to why negotiations don't always lead to behavioral change and many deals that should have been made are not. On the other hand, if you take someone up our stairway, you generate more trust and rapport in a fashion that they're not used to and actually find refreshing. You will, in fairly short order, experience the breakthrough moment of trust-based influence. Not only making deals, but ones that stick. So what does the breakthrough moment look like? It's not when the counterpart says yes. As you will learn in the next few chapters, the breakthrough moment is when your counterpart speaks the sweetest two words in negotiation. That's right. That's right equals green light.
